case_name: Removal of alachlor degradates from agricultural surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing alachlor degradates from surface water intended for drinking water supply under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws water from a river-influenced reservoir in an agricultural catchment where herbicide transformation products are detected during spring and early summer runoff. The utility already operates conventional clarification and filtration and wants a proven upgrade that can reliably reduce alachlor degradates for drinking water compliance and public confidence. Natural organic matter and seasonal turbidity are present, so matrix effects on adsorption and oxidation performance must be considered.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable control of alachlor degradates and associated organic micropollutant risk while meeting normal drinking water treatment expectations for turbidity and microbiological safety.
secondary_goals: use established and defensible treatment technology
fit with existing conventional surface water treatment infrastructure

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: existing sludge handling is available for clarification residuals, but the utility wants to avoid difficult liquid concentrate disposal
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: seasonally robust operation is required during runoff-related contaminant peaks

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize particles and reduce natural organic matter that can compete for downstream adsorption capacity
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form settleable flocs from coagulated particles
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk suspended solids and associated organic matter
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: polish remaining particulates and protect downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb alachlor degradates and other trace organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial inactivation and distribution system protection

applicability_rationale: GAC is a well-established drinking water barrier for organic micropollutants in surface water
existing conventional treatment can be retained and used as effective pretreatment to reduce fouling and competitive adsorption from NOM
the utility prefers proven and robust technology rather than highly specialized advanced treatment
this train avoids high-pressure membranes and difficult concentrate management while directly addressing the primary contaminant class
potential_risks: high DOC and seasonal organic peaks can shorten GAC bed life and require more frequent replacement or reactivation
breakthrough monitoring is needed because degradates may be more mobile than parent pesticides on carbon
upstream clarification performance directly affects carbon run length

core_unit_functions.coagulation: reduce turbidity and part of the organic matrix before oxidation
core_unit_functions.flocculation: grow flocs for efficient solids separation
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove settled floc and lower particle load
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize selected trace organics and improve downstream biodegradability of organic matter
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration_or_gac: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and provide additional adsorption or biofiltration polishing

applicability_rationale: ozonation combined with biologically active filtration is an established advanced treatment approach for surface water organics
this train can provide broader micropollutant control than adsorption alone when oxidizable compounds are present
pretreatment is already conventional and compatible with the utility's current skill set
potential_risks: alachlor degradates may not be removed as predictably by ozone as by well-designed GAC adsorption
bromide in source water introduces bromate formation risk that requires careful ozone control
operator attention is needed for ozone dose control and biofilter management

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: provide flexible episodic adsorption of alachlor degradates during seasonal peaks
core_unit_functions.coagulation: capture PAC and reduce suspended matter and NOM
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form flocs containing PAC and particles
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove PAC-laden floc and clarified solids
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove carryover solids and polish effluent

applicability_rationale: PAC is a conventional add-on approach for seasonal taste, odor, and organic micropollutant events in surface water plants
it can be implemented with existing conventional treatment assets and is useful when contaminant peaks are intermittent
the process is familiar to many operators and avoids high energy demand
potential_risks: PAC performance can be variable for polar degradates and can be strongly reduced by high DOC competition
higher chemical and solids loading increases sludge production
contact time and PAC selection are critical and may be hard to optimize for sustained low-level contaminant control

core_unit_functions.coagulation: reduce colloids and some NOM prior to membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: lower solids loading to downstream membrane units
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration_or_low_pressure_membrane: provide strong particulate and pathogen barrier and stable pretreatment for adsorption
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: remove dissolved alachlor degradates and other trace organics

applicability_rationale: this train offers excellent physical water quality control and protects downstream carbon beds
UF plus GAC is technically sound for surface water with organic micropollutants
it may be considered where treated water stability and pathogen barriers are highly valued
potential_risks: membrane addition increases capital cost and operational complexity beyond what is necessary for an easy case
it does not by itself remove dissolved degradates, so GAC remains essential
membrane fouling control and cleaning requirements add O&M burden

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of dissolved organic contaminants including many pesticide-related compounds
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore finished water chemistry and corrosivity balance after high-rejection membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes are technically capable of controlling dissolved degradates
they may be justified when multiple dissolved contaminants require simultaneous deep removal
this option is included as a lower-ranked technically feasible alternative
potential_risks: concentrate management is inconsistent with the utility's desire to avoid difficult residual streams
energy demand and membrane O&M are unnecessarily high for a case suited to conventional plus adsorption treatment
post-treatment stabilization is required and increases system complexity

preferred_rank_pattern: PAC-based seasonal enhancement can rank below fixed-bed GAC because of less reliable sustained control
high-pressure membrane options should rank last under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints

must_reflect: preference for proven drinking water technologies compatible with existing clarification and filtration
moderate operational capability rather than highly specialized advanced treatment
avoidance of unnecessary energy use and difficult concentrate disposal

must_contain: the role of natural organic matter and turbidity as matrix effects that reduce adsorption efficiency
why GAC is a conventional and well-supported barrier for pesticide-related organic micropollutants
why ozone-based treatment is plausible but less directly supported for alachlor degradates than adsorption
why nanofiltration or reverse osmosis ranks lower despite technical capability

should_mention: GAC breakthrough and media replacement frequency under elevated DOC
bromate formation risk if ozonation is considered
sludge increase with PAC or clarification-intensive approaches
concentrate disposal burden for membrane options
